title: MATBG Josephson diode as an universal thermal machine
abstract: Magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene Josephson junctions (MATBG-JJ) with a gate-tunable valley-polarized weak link exhibit an intrinsic Josephson diode effect originating from broken symmetries associated with valley polarization and band-structure anisotropy. Exploiting this nonreciprocal superconducting platform, we construct quantum Stirling (QSC), Otto (QOC), and Carnot (QCC) thermodynamic cycles, where the valley-polarization potential $Δ_v$ acts as the principal control parameter, in contrast to conventional Josephson thermal machines driven by superconducting phase bias. We systematically compare the performance of MATBG-based Josephson diode thermal machines (MATBG-JDTM) with MATBG-based Josephson junction thermal machines (MATBG-JJTM) and AA-stacked bilayer graphene Josephson junction thermal machines (AABLG-JJTM). Owing to the flat-band-enhanced density of states and electrically tunable nonreciprocal transport in MATBG, both MATBG-JDTM and MATBG-JJTM exhibit significantly enhanced work output and efficiency over a broad operating regime compared to AABLG-JJTM. In particular, the gate-controlled MATBG-JDTM provides a flux-free alternative to conventional phase-driven architectures, mitigating limitations associated with magnetic-flux control and flux-noise effects. Our results establish MATBG Josephson diode platforms as a promising route toward electrically tunable quantum thermal machines and nonreciprocal superconducting caloritronics.
summary: The paper proposes using the intrinsic Josephson diode effect in magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene to create highly efficient, electrically tunable quantum heat engines and refrigerators. By using valley polarization as a control parameter instead of magnetic flux, these devices offer a more stable and efficient alternative to conventional superconducting thermal machines.
main_problem: Investigating the potential of Magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene (MATBG) Josephson diodes to function as universal quantum thermal machines (heat engines and refrigerators) and comparing them to conventional phase-driven architectures.
main_result: MATBG-based thermal machines exhibit significantly enhanced work output and efficiency compared to AA-stacked bilayer graphene due to flat-band-enhanced density of states and trigonal warping. Additionally, the diode-based approach allows for a flux-free, electrically tunable control via valley polarization, mitigating magnetic flux noise.
method: The study uses the Blonder-Tinkham-Klapwijk (BTK) formalism to calculate Andreev bound state (ABS) energies and applies the laws of thermodynamics to analyze Quantum Stirling, Otto, and Carnot cycles.
model_or_system: The system consists of a Magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene (MATBBG) Josephson junction with a gate-tunable, valley-polarized weak link between two superconducting leads, modeled using a 1D effective Hamiltonian incorporating trigonal warping and anisotropic Fermi velocity.
key_observables: Andreev bound state (ABS) spectra, current-phase relations (CPR), work output (W), heat exchange (Q), efficiency (eta), and Coefficient of Performance (COP).
important_parameters: Valley-polarization potential (delta_v), superconducting phase difference (phi), Thouless energy (E_T), trigonal warping scale (E_A), and temperature (T).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a ballistic limit and neglects inter-valley back-scattering; temperatures are assumed to remain below the superconducting critical temperature.
figures_summary: Schematics of the thermal machine loop and junction; ABS spectra and current-phase relations for MATBG and AABLG; plots of work, efficiency, and anomalous current as functions of valley polarization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the MATBG Josephson diode platform, defines the microscopic Hamiltonian and ABS derivation, constructs various quantum thermodynamic cycles, performs a comparative performance analysis against AABLG systems, and discusses experimental advantages regarding flux-free control.
why_interesting: This paper is interesting for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics because it demonstrates how broken symmetries and topological features in a correlated electron system can be harnessed to drive non-equilibrium thermodynamic cycles and control heat/work exchange.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Schematic of a MATBG-JDTM loop with a weak-link valley-polarized region of length d, coupled to thermal baths at temperatures Th and Tc via controllable valves vh and vc. (b) SCL and SCR are the left and right superconducting leads which are characterized by the superconducting order parameter ∆s, and VP is weakly-linked valley-polarized region characterized by potential ∆v which is tunable by external gating. The phase difference across the junction is set to zero, since we are dealing with a Josephson diode (JD).

FIG. 2: Entropy as a function of valley-polarization parameter ∆v for (a) QSC, consisting of two quantum isothermal and two quantum isochoric strokes operating across temperatures Tc and Th, and (b) QOC, consisting of two quantum adiabatic and two quantum isochoric strokes operating across temperatures Tc,Th,Tα and Tβ . Red arrows indicate heat exchange with the hot reservoir, while blue arrows indicate heat exchange with the cold reservoir.

FIG. 3: (a,b) Work and performance metrics of MATBG-JDTM exhibiting a QSC. (c,d) Work and performance metrics of MATBG-JJTM (e,f) for AABLG-JJTM for a QSC, in heat engine (HE), refrigerator (RE), cold pump (CP), and Joule pump (JP) regimes. The bath temperatures are Th = 0.1K and Tc = 0.05K.

FIG. 4: (a,b) Work and performance metrics of MATBG-JDTM exhibiting a QOC. (c,d) Work and performance metrics of MATBG-JJTM (e,f) for AABLG-JJTM for a QOC, in the heat engine (HE), refrigerator (RE), and cold pump (CP) regimes. The bath temperatures are Th = 0.1K and Tc = 0.05K.
FIG. 5: (a) Isothermal contour plots depicting QSC in MATB-JDTM, (b),(c) are isoentropic contours for MATBG-JDTM depicting QOC and QCC, respectively.
